speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape three. When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: In April 1954.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lena.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We have two. We have a daughter, Dorothy, who's married and has two children, our two grandchildren. And we have a son who's about to get married in the next few months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever talk to your children about the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We do talk. But we don't make a career out of talking and discussing it. They are-- they're well aware what my wife and I went through. And we taught them to understand what happened to us. But whatever we taught them-- we taught them not to hate. Maybe they should-- they should remember what happened to us. And hopefully it will never happen again to them or to anybody else. But we tried-- and we did try to lead a normal life and bring them up in normal circumstances, give them best education and love. And I think it worked out for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think your experiences have changed you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, maybe more sensitive to, to, to other-- to other people's feelings. By having seen so much brutality and so much blood and so much tears, that one would think that would make you more immune and to me, it made me more sensitive. I think I'm more sensitive to blood, and to murder, and to crime than maybe than a normal person. Although I've seen this for four years day after day, to my own relatives, my own friends. So maybe that's-- in that way it changed me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever met some of your ex camp survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: I meet quite often, quite often. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: They your friends today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they are friends. Some of them are closer friends. Some are not.

speaker: interviewer
text: A lot of them down here in Miami?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a lot. No. But there are a few. There are a few.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you tell the story before?

speaker: interviewee
text: To tell-- the public place--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like this story, sort of this format, or-- yes, in a public place. Did you ever make a speech about it or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was interviewed after the the movie, came out. I was interviewed once at a speech in front of a high school in Boca Raton. And there was a question and answer period. I was there. There was-- that they filmed that. And a few weeks ago, I was interviewed for NBC in Atlanta after this new book, came out, which retells the story of 20-some-odd survivors . I was one among them. So they take him down here, they interviewed me after they had read-- they had read this book.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you do this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I want it preserved. I wanted people to know what happened. And maybe if more people are aware of, of these terrible things that happened, maybe they would prevent them happening in the future. So maybe my children and my grandchildren and their children, their grandchildren will never-- will never have to experience what we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My will to live, I think. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was always-- I didn't leave-- I didn't live in a dream world. But somehow I thought always there's another day. Maybe, well, just like somebody who sits in jail and marks the days, how many days he has left to sit in jail. With me, I was mentally marking off the day as well. Another day passed, so we're still alive. Because you didn't make obviously any plans for any, any future, having lost your family, everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that your worst memory?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's, that's not a question-- memory. This was like a part of my life. It's not even memory. It's there. It's-- doesn't go away. But on the other hand, I'm not thinking 24 hours a day about it. It's there. It's like a computer, if you press it, it's there. If you don't press it, it's just thought.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel about the way you lived your life? And what's important to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, important to me was-- family was number one. Always, always. Since having lost family. So this was like a-- I happened-- I was lucky enough to find-- to find the girl who became my wife, who had the same values that I-- so we both cherished. So we-- we tried to stay close to our family, to give them the best education, and give them different values in life, whatever we could afford.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, when we are old, we are very happy because we are very close with them. They still go with us on vacations. And we try to enjoy life from day to-- every day, every day. We do a lot of things together. I mean my wife and I, and also with our children and grandchildren, et cetera. Have there been any decisions you've made or things that you've done that were directly influenced by the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the only decisions-- maybe there was the decision that-- not to be obsessed with it 24 hours a day. I mean if that's-- that's the only decision.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you would like us to note that I did not ask?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like them to note that, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that we lead a very nice life, my wife and I. We both-- even though I'm 70 and she's in her 60s, we still enjoy a lot of sports. We play tennis, we, we ski, we, we swim, we, we go dancing. And we have a lot of friends and parties. And we really try to-- every day we try to enjoy. We live here in Florida now. And in the wintertime, we have a nice home in the woods in the mountains in New York. And like I said, every day we enjoy it because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this is our life. That's how we want to pursue it.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's beautiful. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Would you care to introduce your wife to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd be more than happy to. This is Lena, my wife of 40 years. And she has a happy look on her face. I don't know. Maybe it worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Lena is also from Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm from Poland. I'm from Drohobych, which is neighbor, twin city of Boryslaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the United States? OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] These four pages constitute the entire telephone book of the city of Boryslaw in 1935. A family who immigrated to Sweden saved this book and they sent us last year those four pages. Among those names is our telephone. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] As you can see it, it says the first name. It says Nadel Katz, [INAUDIBLE]. That means that's the firm of which my father owned, owned partially. And also states his name A. Klinghoffer, prok, which means chief executive officer. And then it says m, which means [INAUDIBLE], means the living quarters. Wodna, that was the street, number 5. The phone number was 1345. Maybe you want to see the picture of my father. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is the only picture of my father, Arnold Klinghoffer. This picture was taken about 1937, or '38. And it's the only remaining picture that I have and cherish.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get that picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was a part of a group picture taken at the commemoration of-- actual of celebration of my father's 25th anniversary of, of, of managing the-- this company. And he was sitting among other associates. And there was-- this photograph was taken out of that picture. And somebody who survived had this picture, sent me, and I had a reproduction made. I had to produce this picture. And this picture I am here with my sister, Helena. This picture was taken in 1975-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1980, I'm sorry. 1980

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: At a party in New York. This is I, Igor Kling, and Helena Kling-Osterreicher, her married name. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In summer of 1993, I, Igor Kling, went with my son back to Boryslaw, formerly Poland, and visited our house. Here I'm standing in front of the house, which is dilapidated now and neglected. This is a picture of our gymnasium, high school, in Boryslaw, Poland, which I attended for-- over 50 years ago. This picture was taken in 1993. The building still stands and is preserved in fairly good condition.

speaker: interviewer
text: Still a school? Still a school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and it still functions as a high school. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'll start it right there. Here is I, Igor Kling, with our son, Arnold, on our visit in 1993 to Boryslaw. We are standing in front of [INAUDIBLE], which was the camp, so-called last bastion of Jews who's-- who was still there before the last transport. We were about 1,000 Jews left over from over 14,000 when the war started. And from there we went to Plazow, and that was the end of Jews in Boryslaw until the end of the war. Above us, as you can see, there's a plaque put up by the Russians, a small plaque that just commemorates those who were there incarcerated for, for a time being. That plaque is-- This is a commemorative plaque put up by the Russians on the [INAUDIBLE], the camp in Boryslaw. This plaque was put up sometime after the war. This picture was taken in 1993.

speaker: interviewer
text: Note to the video editor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Note to the video editor. This plaque is in [INAUDIBLE] which is near Boryslaw. And there was a-- there was a mass slaughter of Jews, and that-- there was a monument put up in the last couple of years. And that plaque commemorates these who died there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, the first description was incorrect.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: The first description refers to this plaque, which we'll now describe again. OK, now we're talking about the top one.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. This plaque is on the wall of [INAUDIBLE], which was the camp where the leftovers, the, the, the last part of Jews from the city in Boryslaw were staying there until their transport to Plazow. After we were taken from there, the [INAUDIBLE] was left, abandoned. And there abandoned until this day. Here is a picture--

speaker: interviewer
text: One second. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is [INAUDIBLE] camp in Boryslaw, Poland from where the last part of Jews was transported to concentration camps in 1944. And the buildings, as dilapidated as it is, was abandoned. And it's falling apart. Nothing is done to it to preserve it or to rebuild it. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is our old sign informing us that we are entering the town of Boryslaw. It's spelling in Ukrainian 'Boryslaw' in the background.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here, I, Igor Kling, with a-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This is a-- this is a sign written in Ukrainian, 'Boryslaw,' in blue letters in the background. And in front of it I am on the left, Igor Kling. And next to me is Maurice Najgel and Charlotte Hacker, who were on our-- on the same trip with us from the United States. This picture was taken in 1993. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] In this picture, Lenna and Igor Kling are at a dinner in May 1994, honoring Mrs. Amelia Schindler, wife of Oskar Schindler, in Miami.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is a New Year's picture taken in 1994 in Switzerland. And there are, from left to right, Lena Kling, Dorothy Kling-Weinsten, Arnold Kling and his fiancee, Nina Welch, and I, Igor Kling. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is I, Igor Kling, celebrating my 70th birthday on skis with my 7-year-old son, Charles Weinstein--

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandson.

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandson, I'm sorry-- Charles Weinstein. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is our granddaughter, Alissa Weinstein, 10 years old. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This is our grandson, Charles Weinstein, 7 years old. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] These are our grandchildren, Alissa Weinstein and Charles Weinstein, 10 years and 7 years old, respectively.
